name: command-management
description: Use PROACTIVELY this skill when you need to create or update custom commands following best practices
capabilities: Create personal slash commands available across all projects in ~/.claude/commands/
Follow standardized template with YAML frontmatter and workflow sections
Test created commands using the SlashCommand tool
Organize commands using namespacing and subdirectories
Configure allowed tools and permissions for each command
author: Emz1998
securitySummary: This is a documentation and template skill with no executable code. Static analysis flagged 147 potential issues in documentation files (command examples, template placeholders, bash execution syntax). All findings are FALSE POSITIVES - the analyzer misidentified documentation examples and template syntax as security patterns. This skill only contains markdown documentation files that define command templates and usage guidelines.
valueStatement: Developers spend time recreating common prompts and workflows. This skill provides templates and guidance for creating reusable slash commands in Claude Code, reducing repetitive work and standardizing team workflows.
limitations: Does not execute actual bash commands - only creates command files
Commands are limited to Claude Code environment
Does not provide command implementation logic

useCases.title: Team workflow standardization
useCases.description: Create consistent commands for common team workflows like code review, commit messages, or testing procedures. All team members use the same prompts with standardized outputs.

useCases.title: Personal productivity automation
useCases.description: Build personal commands for frequently used tasks like generating boilerplate code, running diagnostics, or creating documentation templates across any project.

useCases.title: Onboarding and documentation
useCases.description: Create commands that guide new team members through setup procedures, coding standards reviews, or deployment processes with consistent step-by-step instructions.

promptTemplates.title: Basic command creation
promptTemplates.prompt: Create a new slash command called /optimize that analyzes code for performance issues and suggests optimizations. Use the command-management skill template.
promptTemplates.scenario: Creating a simple command for code optimization

promptTemplates.title: Git workflow command
promptTemplates.prompt: Create a project command /commit that helps create git commits. Include git status, diff review, and commit message generation. Configure Bash tool permissions for git operations.
promptTemplates.scenario: Building a command for git commit workflow

promptTemplates.title: Personal cross-project command
promptTemplates.prompt: Create a personal command /security-review that reviews code for security vulnerabilities. Save it to the user commands directory so it works across all my projects.
promptTemplates.scenario: Creating a personal command available in any project

promptTemplates.title: Multi-phase workflow command
promptTemplates.prompt: Create a complex command /deploy that handles the deployment process. It should have three phases: pre-deployment checks, deployment execution, and post-deployment validation. Use phases and acceptance criteria.
promptTemplates.scenario: Building a multi-phase command with workflow stages

bestPractices: Keep command descriptions concise and action-oriented
Use the YAML frontmatter to define allowed tools explicitly for security
Test commands with the SlashCommand tool before deployment
antiPatterns: Avoid creating commands without proper YAML frontmatter
Do not save commands outside the .claude/commands/ or ~/.claude/commands/ directories
Avoid granting excessive tool permissions - use least-privilege principle

faq.question: Where are custom slash commands stored?
faq.answer: Project commands go in .claude/commands/ within your repository. Personal commands go in ~/.claude/commands/ in your home directory and are available across all projects.

faq.question: How do I test a newly created command?
faq.answer: Use the SlashCommand tool with the command name. For example, run /SlashCommand name="/your-command" to test. You can also trigger it naturally by referencing the command in conversation.

faq.question: Can I use arguments in slash commands?
faq.answer: Yes. Use $ARGUMENTS for all arguments or $1, $2, etc. for individual positional parameters. Define argument hints in the frontmatter for user guidance.

faq.question: How do I restrict which tools a command can use?
faq.answer: Add the allowed-tools field in the YAML frontmatter. List specific tools with optional wildcards like Bash(git add:*). This follows the principle of least privilege.

faq.question: What happens if I create a command with the same name as a built-in command?
faq.answer: Custom commands cannot override built-in slash commands. Use unique names for your custom commands to avoid conflicts.

faq.question: Can I organize commands into categories?
faq.answer: Yes. Create subdirectories within .claude/commands/ for organization. The subdirectory name appears in the command description but does not affect the command name.
